videos.title: Bogotá: The Don'ts of Visiting Bogota, Colombia
videos.description:
Are you heading to Bogotá, Colombia and not sure what to expect? Well here we go through the do's and don'ts of visiting Bogotá! Whether you are debating the safety of Bogota (don't worry, it is not the 1980s anymore) or debating on the food to eat (Ajiaco is the local soup you need to try) to figuring out which seats to not sit in on the Transmilenio (don't sit in the blue ones) we have honest travel advice for your Colombian vacation in Bogota. 
Filmed in Bogota, Colombia

videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Bogotá, and in this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here. So hope you enjoy it! Bogotá is located high in the Andes mountains in the center of Colombia. It's the vibrant capital and the largest city in the country. Known for its diverse neighborhoods, warm hospitality, and rich culinary scene, Bogotá brings together the essence of Colombia. In this video, we highlight 10 of the best things to do in and around Bogotá. At the end, we'll also share a few travel tips as well as safety recommendations. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Bolivar square is located in the heart of La Candelaria District. It's named after the famous Simon Bolivar, who was the Liberator of South America. Some of the iconic structures surrounding the square include the Palace of Justice, City Hall, and the cathedral. It's an excellent place to observe the daily life of Bogotá and enjoy cultural events, which are frequently held here. Above all, it's a busy area area with lots of street vendors lining the sidewalks right next to it. Bogotá offers a variety of delicious dishes that reflect the rich cultural heritage of Colombia. One of of the must-tries in the city is ajiaco. This is a hearty soup made with chicken, potatoes, corn, and avocado. Bandeja Paisa is a heavier option that features rice, beans, lots of different meats, avocado, plantain, and arepa. Besides that, Bogotá also offers a thriving street food scene, consisting of arepas, empanadas, tamales, and a range of delicious tropical fruits. Check out the link to our Google Map in the description below the video in that map you'll find a curated list of recommendations on places to eat and drink around the city. Tejo is the national sport of Colombia based on a game that was developed by indigenous people in pre-Columbian times. This game involves throwing metal discs at targets in a clay-filled box. the goal is to hit as many of the targets as possible to gain points and win the game. And while tejo is typically played in outdoor spaces with music, beers, and food, there are also many bars that offer a traditional experience. One of the interesting things is that these targets contain gunpowder, so when you hit them it actually explodes. If you only have time to visit one museum in Bogotá, then the Gold Museum should be it. Located in the heart of the city, it houses the world's largest collection of pre-Hispanic gold artifacts, showcasing the incredible craftsmanship and artistry of ancient Colombian cultures. Many of these items were used in daily life or during sacred rituals of indigenous people. There are different exhibits with information in English and Spanish. These explain the remarkable histories of the various indigenous groups in Colombia. Situated on the edge of the city, Monserrate is a tall mountain that towers above the city center. The easiest way to get here is by taking the cable car, but if you want a challenge it's also possible to hike up a trail with hundreds of steps. Once you get to the summit, we recommend visiting the church, dining at one of the gourmet restaurants, or simply enjoying the panoramic views of the city. It's best to visit on a weekday in the morning or an hour before sunset. If you visit at golden hour, you can catch amazing views of the city skyline. If you drive just an hour outside of Bogotá, you can find Colombia's tallest waterfall. These falls are 590 meters or 1,935 feet tall. The hike to get to the falls starts at La Chorrera Park, known for its diverse range of bird and plant life. Then, you follow a 5.8 kilometers or 3.6 miles long loop trail with reasonable elevation gain. On the hike, you'll enjoy amazing views of the valley. You'll also witness the El Chiflon waterfall which you can even walk behind. The icing on the cake is the La Chorrera Falls, where you can feel the water next to you from the viewing platform. Since it's impossible to reach the park by public transportation, you need to book a guided tour. You can find a link to the tour we joined in the description below the video. Another great day trip from Bogotá goes to the nearby town of Zipaquirá. Here, you can find one of the 'Seven Wonders of Colombia': the Salt Cathedral. Built within the tunnels of a salt mine about 180 meters or 600 feet below a mountain, it's a monument of both religious devotion and architectural innovation. When entering the cathedral, illuminated tunnels first lead you through the Stations of the Cross. At the end, you'll find three separate naves that represent key moments in Jesus's life. Each nave has beautiful sculptures carved by miners and artists. There's also a short light and sound show in the main nave. The tour we joined included a free audio guide, a 3D movie that explains the history of the salt mines, and a quick trip around the historic city center of Zipaquirá. Situated in the heart of Bogotá, the Botanical Garden is home to a diverse collection of plants from around the world. It's a tranquil oasis with a range of manicured walking paths and interesting features, such as a waterfall, pond, and bridge. The most interesting attraction is its Tropicario, a structure made up of six domes where you can experience the bioclimatic conditions of various ecosystems in Colombia. You travel through different parts of the country here, from the tropical rainforest to the threatened tropical dry forest. It's also fascinating to learn about the usage of plants as food, medicine, and fabric development. It's a great introduction to the biodiversity of the country. At the time of our visit, the entrance fee was 27,000 Colombian Pesos or about 6 US Dollars. The Botanical Gardens here are definitely a highlight for us in the city, but if you're looking for a free alternative, you can also go to the nearby Simon Bolivar Park. It's got a lot more open space. La Candelaria is Bogotá's historic heart, dating back to the 16th century. This vibrant neighborhood is filled with colonial architecture, art, and culture. Wander through its charming cobblestone lanes, admire the street art, and soak up the lively atmosphere. When the sun goes down, the neighborhood truly comes alive, with the local spirit called "chicha" being sold on every street corner. The area is also home to a wide range of restaurants, cafés, and bars, where you can try the delicious local cuisine. These are the reasons why most travelers decide to stay in La Candelaria when visiting Bogotá. Bogotá is known as a bicycle-friendly city, with lots of dedicated cycling paths. As such, one of one of the best ways to explore the city is on bike. On Sundays, some of the main traffic roads are closed to cars and turned into cycleways for bikes and pedestrians. This transformation of the city infrastructure for the day is called "ciclovía". We decided to join a guided bike tour on that day, which started in La Candelaria and then took us around different neighborhoods of Bogotá. There were plenty of stops including a small coffee factory, a traditional fruit market, an art workshop, many parks and plazas. Along the way, our guide explained Bogotá's interesting history. At the end, there was also some time for a game of tejo with our group. The tour was about 4 hours long with mostly flat terrain. And while we chose to do this activity on Sunday, it's also possible to do it on any other day of the week. You can find a link to the tour we joined in the description below the video. As we promised at the beginning of the video, we'll now share a few travel and safety tips for Bogotá. First, we recommend learning basic Spanish phrases. While tour guides and some people in tourist areas speak English, knowing a few phrases in the local language is is essential. Downloading an offline translation App such as Google Translate might come in handy as well. A popular and convenient way to get around the city is with Uber, especially for short distances and in safe areas. You can also book official yellow taxis through the Uber App. Avoid unofficial taxis as they may overcharge or pose safety risks. Bogotá is situated at a high altitude of 2,625 meters or 8,612 feet. To acclimate, you should drink plenty of water and avoid strenuous activities in the first few days. Also be aware that Bogotá's weather can be unpredictable with sudden rain showers. Pack a rain jacket and sweater as it gets cold in the evenings. We also recommend keeping valuables hidden and avoiding flashing them in public. This is an expression known locally as "no dar papaya". You should always stay alert, especially in crowded areas. Avoid walking alone in dark and deserted areas, especially at night. La Candelaria in particular can feel a bit unsafe at night if you're on the less popular streets. We stayed in La Candelaria, which is a very central location. However, like we mentioned before some areas can be less safe here at night. You will also find lots of homelessness in and around parks. Alternatively, stay in the upscale Chapinero or Teusaquillo. While Chapinero is a bit further away from the tourist center, Teusaquillo is a quieter neighborhood with a more residential feel. We left a few links to good accommodation options in the description below this video. If you're planning your trip to Colombia, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below the video. We also included other important links in the description that will help with your Bogotá experience, such as an interactive map so you can get to each place we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!
